article_meta.title: Historic pilgrimage for LGBTQ+ Catholics at Vatican6 September 2025ShareSaveJaroslav LukivBBC NewsShareSaveAFP via Getty

transcript.content:
You are a news summarization assistant. Summarize the following article and extract its key facts. Respond ONLY with a valid JSON object — no prose, no markdown fences:
{
  "summary": "string (150-200 words)",
  "key_facts": ["string","string","string","string","string"],
  "sources_represented": ["string"]
}
Rules:
- summary must be 150-200 words
- key_facts must contain EXACTLY 5 items
- sources_represented must list every distinct perspective included

Summarize this article:

---
HEADLINE: Historic pilgrimage for LGBTQ+ Catholics at Vatican6 September 2025ShareSaveJaroslav LukivBBC NewsShareSaveAFP via Getty
SOURCE: BBC Fact Check

ARTICLE:

Historic pilgrimage for LGBTQ+ Catholics at Vatican6 September 2025ShareSaveJaroslav LukivBBC NewsShareSaveAFP via Getty ImagesPilgrims march to pass the holy door of St Peter's basilica during the LGBT jubilee, at the VaticanSome 1,400 Catholics donning rainbow attire and carrying crosses are taking part in the first officially recognised LGBTQ+ pilgrimage to Rome as part of the Vatican's Jubilee Year. Coming from 20 countries, pilgrims are attending prayer vigils, masses and other activities this weekend - though they will not have a private audience with Pope Leo XIV. His predecessor Pope Francis, who died in April, did not change the Roman Catholic Church doctrine regarding the LGBTQ+ community - but made overtures in a decree in 2023.These included allowing priests to bless same-sex couples - a move that angered conservatives Catholics, notably in Africa.On Saturday, members from the LGBTQ+ community entered St Peter's Basilica though its Holy Door - a procession that symbolises reconciliation.The door only opens once in 25 years to mark the Roman Catholic Church's jubilee years."Not only are LGBTQ people marching and walking to say that they're part of the Church, but official Church institutions are welcoming them and helping them to tell their stories," Francis DeBernardo, executive director of New Ways Ministry, was quoted as saying by the National Catholic Reporter website.The ministry helps advocate for the LGBTQ+ community within the Catholic Church. NurPhoto via Getty ImagesSome 32 million pilgrims are expected to descend on the Vatican this year for the Jubilee celebrations.Pope Leo, who was elected in May, has yet to address the LGBTQ+ community publicly.The American pontiff has also not commented on his predecessor's 2023 decree.In 2020, Pope Francis said "homosexual people have a right to be in a family"."They are children of God... nobody should be thrown out or made miserable over it", he said.Three years later, Francis allowed priests to bless same-sex and "irregular" couples, under certain circumstances.But the Vatican said such blessings should not be part of regular Church rituals or related to civil unions or weddings.It added that it continued to view marriage as between a man and a woman.Pope says priests can bless same-sex couplesCatholicismPope FrancisRoman Catholic ChurchSame-sex marriagePope Leo XIVLGBTRelatedPope appoints new leader of Catholic Church in England and WalesPope Leo names Ronald Hicks next archbishop of New YorkPope Leo visits Istanbul's Blue MosqueMore from the BBC1 day agoSpain's Catholic Church signs deal on sexual abuse compensationThe accord follows complaints that religious leaders had failed to tackle the issue adequately.1 day ago31 Dec 2025Notable deaths 2025Pope Francis, Ozzy Osbourne and Diane Keaton are among those who died this year.31 Dec 202530 Dec 2025Honour for LGBTQ+ campaign is 'humbling'Guernsey deputy awarded OBE for services to religion and LGBTQ+ campaigns says honour is 'humbling'.30 Dec 202530 Dec 2025Spanish woman who found fame for botching fresco restoration diesCecilia Giménez's effort to restore the painting Ecce Homo earned the artwork the nickname "Monkey Christ".30 Dec 202529 Dec 2025Man appointed MBE for services to LGBTQ+ communityMatt Daniels says the award is a "huge honour". 29 Dec 2025
---

{
  "summary": "In a landmark event during the Vatican's Jubilee Year, about 1,400 LGBTQ+ Catholics from 20 countries undertook the first officially recognized LGBTQ+ pilgrimage to Rome. Participants donned rainbow colors and carried crosses, joining prayer vigils, Masses, and activities, though they did not receive a private audience with the newly elected Pope Leo XIV. The pilgrimage included a procession through St Peter’s Basilica’s Holy Door, which opens only every 25 years for jubilee celebrations, symbolizing reconciliation. The event marks a shift towards greater inclusivity in the Catholic Church, albeit with ongoing debates; Pope Francis, who died in April 2025, had allowed blessings of same-sex couples but maintained that such blessings should not equate to marriage or be part of official ceremonies, leading to backlash from some conservative Catholics, particularly in Africa. Pope Leo XIV has yet to address LGBTQ+ issues publicly or comment on his predecessor’s decree. Advocates, including organizations like New Ways Ministry, see this pilgrimage as a sign that the Church is making incremental progress towards embracing LGBTQ+ members.",
  "key_facts": [
    "1,400 LGBTQ+ Catholics participated in the first officially recognized pilgrimage to the Vatican during Jubilee Year 2025.",
    "Pilgrims from 20 countries took part in Masses, vigils, and a procession through the Holy Door of St Peter’s Basilica.",
    "Pope Leo XIV, elected in May 2025, has not addressed LGBTQ+ issues or commented on past decrees by Pope Francis.",
    "Pope Francis allowed priests to bless same-sex couples in 2023, but church doctrine and marriage teachings remain unchanged.",
    "The event was supported by advocacy groups such as New Ways Ministry and met both support and opposition within the Church."
  ],
  "sources_represented": [
    "BBC News",
    "National Catholic Reporter",
    "New Ways Ministry"
  ]
}

parsed_output.summary: In a landmark event during the Vatican's Jubilee Year, about 1,400 LGBTQ+ Catholics from 20 countries undertook the first officially recognized LGBTQ+ pilgrimage to Rome. Participants donned rainbow colors and carried crosses, joining prayer vigils, Masses, and activities, though they did not receive a private audience with the newly elected Pope Leo XIV. The pilgrimage included a procession through St Peter’s Basilica’s Holy Door, which opens only every 25 years for jubilee celebrations, symbolizing reconciliation. The event marks a shift towards greater inclusivity in the Catholic Church, albeit with ongoing debates; Pope Francis, who died in April 2025, had allowed blessings of same-sex couples but maintained that such blessings should not equate to marriage or be part of official ceremonies, leading to backlash from some conservative Catholics, particularly in Africa. Pope Leo XIV has yet to address LGBTQ+ issues publicly or comment on his predecessor’s decree. Advocates, including organizations like New Ways Ministry, see this pilgrimage as a sign that the Church is making incremental progress towards embracing LGBTQ+ members.
key_facts: 1,400 LGBTQ+ Catholics participated in the first officially recognized pilgrimage to the Vatican during Jubilee Year 2025.
Pilgrims from 20 countries took part in Masses, vigils, and a procession through the Holy Door of St Peter’s Basilica.
Pope Leo XIV, elected in May 2025, has not addressed LGBTQ+ issues or commented on past decrees by Pope Francis.
Pope Francis allowed priests to bless same-sex couples in 2023, but church doctrine and marriage teachings remain unchanged.
The event was supported by advocacy groups such as New Ways Ministry and met both support and opposition within the Church.
